blocks.text: The structure of Salah is divinely ordained, consisting of specific units known as Rakaat. Each Rakaat is a journey through different states of submission: standing, bowing, and prostrating. Understanding the number of Rakaat for each prayer is the first step in mastery, but recognizing the wisdom behind the different types of prayers—Fardh, Sunnah, and Nafl—is what allows a believer to build a complete and resilient spiritual life.

The Fardh prayers are the non-negotiable core of a Muslim's duty. Fajr consists of 2 Rakaat, Dhuhr of 4, Asr of 4, Maghrib of 3, and Isha of 4. These seventeen Rakaat throughout the day are the absolute minimum required to maintain the covenant with Allah. They are the 'debts' we owe to our Creator, and fulfilling them is the baseline of our faith.

Beyond the Fardh are the Sunnah and Nafl prayers. Sunnah Mu'akkadah (emphasized Sunnah) are those the Prophet ﷺ almost never missed, such as the 2 Rakaat before Fajr or the 2 after Maghrib. Nafl prayers are purely voluntary. The wisdom of these extra prayers is profound: they serve as a 'buffer' and a 'repair kit.' They fill in the gaps and deficiencies of our Fardh prayers, such as moments when our Khushu wavered or our concentration slipped.

blocks.textEn: Narrated `Aisha:the mother of believers: Allah enjoined the prayer and when He enjoined it, it was two rak`at only (in every prayer) both when in residence or on journey. Then the prayers offered on journey remained the same, but (the rak`at of) the prayers for non-travelers were increased

blocks.text: This hadith provides foundational knowledge on how the prayer was originally prescribed and how its units were established. It highlights that the structure we follow today is rooted in the direct guidance given to the Prophet ﷺ. Each Rakaat we perform is a link in a chain of worship that goes back to the very beginning of the Prophetic mission.

blocks.textEn: Umm Habiba (the wife of the Holy Prophet) reported Allah's Messenger (ﷺ) as saying:A house will be built in Paradise, for anyone who prays in a day and a night twelve rak'ahs; and she added: I have never abandoned (observing them) since I heard it from the Messenger of Allah (ﷺ). Some of the other narrators said the same words: I have never abandoned (observing them) since I heard (from so and so)

blocks.text: The Prophet ﷺ emphasized the immense virtue of the Sunnah prayers, stating that for whoever prays twelve Rakaat of Sunnah in a day and night, a house will be built for them in Paradise. These twelve include: 4 before Dhuhr and 2 after, 2 after Maghrib, 2 after Isha, and 2 before Fajr. This promise of an eternal home encourages us to go beyond the bare minimum and strive for excellence in our worship.

If you find it difficult to maintain Khushu in your Fardh prayers, try to consistently pray the Sunnah before them. These extra Rakaat act as a 'warm-up' for the heart, allowing you to settle your mind and leave the world behind before you begin the obligatory prayer.

Start by being consistent with your Fardh prayers. Once they are established, gradually add the Sunnah Mu'akkadah, starting with the two Rakaat before Fajr, which the Prophet ﷺ said are 'better than the world and everything in it.' This gradual progression builds a sustainable habit of worship that becomes the backbone of your day.

Ultimately, the variety in Rakaat and prayer types is a mercy from Allah. It allows us to increase our reward and fix our mistakes. When you stand for your Fardh prayer after having prayed the Sunnah, you do so with a heart already softened and a mind already focused, paving the way for a deeper and more meaningful Khushu.
